title: Time-Multiplexed Distributed Quantum Sensing
summary_bullets: The paper introduces a time-multiplexed distributed quantum sensing protocol that exploits entanglement across temporal modes to asymptotically realize simultaneous Heisenberg scaling in photons (N), spatial modes (M), and measurement repetitions (R), yielding Δ^2ϕ ∝ 1/(N M R)^2 in the large-R limit. Using a Bogoliubov-transformation analysis of multimode Gaussian probes the authors prove optimality within the class of Gaussian states, derive the quantum Fisher information / QCRB for the protocol, and identify homodyne detection plus maximum-likelihood estimation as an implementable, QCRB‑saturating measurement. They further analyze the effect of optical loss (giving closed-form lossy QFIM results), show the advantage survives realistic loss regimes, and propose a loop-based photonic implementation compatible with existing time-domain multiplexing technology.

This is directly relevant to Yanbei’s expertise in quantum measurement theory and optomechanics because it offers a route to improve sensitivity by harnessing temporal-mode entanglement rather than increasing instantaneous optical power—useful for experiments constrained by quantum back-action or photodamage and for distributed/weak signals (e.g., gravity‑coupled effects) where repeated low‑power probes are standard. The Gaussian/Bogoliubov formalism and QFIM/QCRB analysis align with his prior work on quantum noise, QND/back‑action‑evading strategies, and continuous‑variable networks, and the homodyne + MLE readout and loop‑based photonic architecture suggest concrete paths to integrate with interferometric or optomechanical platforms probing gravity‑induced decoherence or entanglement.

Keywords/topics: time-multiplexed distributed quantum sensing, temporal‑mode entanglement, Heisenberg scaling in repetitions, Gaussian probes, Bogoliubov transformation, QFIM/QCRB, homodyne detection, loss robustness, loop‑based photonic implementation; Published on arXiv (v1) 19–20 March 2026 — very recent.
